text: jointdata object list consisting three data-frames, collectively contain repeated measurement data, time--event data baseline covariate data. repeated measurement data must stored unbalanced format. time--event baseline covariate data must stored balanced format, .e. one line per subject. data-frame must include column containing subject id, three subject id columns must match. UniqueVariables function provides convenient way extract time--event base line covariate data unbalanced data-frame, following example. second example, create jointdata object single data-frame heart.valve follows: summary jointdata object can obtained using summary function. example: jointdata object can also constructed specified subset subjects. example, selects subjects whose survival status zero, .e. event-time right-censored. sample function can also used select random sample subjects jointdata object, example: takes data random sample 10 150 subjects assigns result jointdata object named heart.jd.sample.
code: liver.long <- liver[, 1:3] liver.surv <- UniqueVariables(liver, var.col = c("survival", "cens"),                                id.col = "id") liver.baseline <- UniqueVariables(liver, var.col = 4,                                   id.col = "id")  liver.jd <- jointdata(longitudinal = liver.long,                       survival = liver.surv,                        baseline = liver.baseline,                       id.col = "id",                       time.col = "time") heart.surv <- UniqueVariables(heart.valve, var.col = c("fuyrs", "status"),                               id.col = "num") heart.long <- heart.valve[, c(1, 4, 5, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11)] heart.jd <- jointdata(longitudinal = heart.long,                        survival = heart.surv,                       id.col = "num",                       time.col = "time") summary(heart.jd) ## $subjects ## [1] "Number of subjects: 256" ##  ## $longitudinal ##            class ## time     numeric ## fuyrs    numeric ## grad     numeric ## log.grad numeric ## lvmi     numeric ## log.lvmi numeric ## ef       integer ##  ## $survival ##                                   ## Number of subjects that fail:  54 ## Number of subjects censored:  202 ##  ## $baseline ## [1] "No baseline covariates data available" ##  ## $times ## [1] "Unbalanced longitudinal study or more than twenty observation times" take <- heart.jd$survival$num[heart.jd$survival$status == 0] heart.jd.cens <- subset(heart.jd, take) set.seed(94561) heart.jd.sample <- sample.jointdata(heart.jd, size = 10)

hi(t)=h0(t)exp⁡{Xi2⊤β2+γ(U0i+U1it+U2it2)}\begin{eqnarray*}   \mbox{longitudinal sub-model:  } & Y_{ij} = X_{i1}^\top \beta_1 + U_{0i} + U_{1i}t_{ij} + U_{2i}{t_{ij}}^2 + Z_{ij} \\   \mbox{hazard sub-model:  }& h_i(t) = h_0(t) \exp{\{ X_{i2}^\top \beta_2 + \gamma\left(U_{0i} + U_{1i}t + U_{2i}t^2\right) \}} \\ \end{eqnarray*} argument sepassoc can set TRUE model choices, allowing different hi(t)=h0(t)exp⁡{Xi2⊤β2+γ0U0i+γ1U1it}\begin{eqnarray*}   \mbox{longitudinal sub-model:  } & Y_{ij} = X_{i1}^\top \beta_1 + U_{0i} + U_{1i}t_{ij} + Z_{ij} \\   \mbox{hazard sub-model:  }& h_i(t) = h_0(t) \exp{\{ X_{i2}^\top \beta_2 + \gamma_0 U_{0i} + \gamma_1 U_{1i}t \}} \\ \end{eqnarray*} default joineR sepassoc = FALSE, case model proportional association used. now consider example separate title: Joint Modelling of Repeated Measurements and Time-to-Event Data
text: joineR package implements methods analyzing data longitudinal studies response subject consists time-sequence repeated measurements possibly censored time--event outcome. modelling framework repeated measurements linear model random effects /correlated error structure (Laird Ware, 1982). model time--event outcome Cox proportional hazards model log-Gaussian frailty (Cox, 1972). Stochastic dependence captured allowing Gaussian random effects linear model correlated frailty term Cox proportional hazards model. methodology used fit model described Henderson et al. (2002) Wulfsohn Tsiatis (1997). joineR package also allows competing risks data jointly modelled cause-specific hazards model. importance accounting competing risks detailed Williamson et al. (2007a,b). methodology used fit model described Williamson et al. (2008).

title: Joint Modelling of Repeated Measurements and Time-to-Event Data
text: joineR package comes several data sets including one describes survival patients underwent aortic valve replacement surgery. patients routinely followed clinic, left ventricular mass index (LVMI) calculated. fit joint model, must first create jointdata object, holds survival, longitudinal, baseline covariate data, along names columns identify patient identifiers repeated time outcomes. creation heart.valve.jd object, can fit joint model using joint function. , need 4 arguments: jointdata: data object created long.formula: linear mixed effects model formula longitudinal sub-model surv.formula: survival formula survival sub-model model: latent association structure. Full details data functions provided help documentation package vignette. purpose code simply illustrate ease speed fitting models.
code: library(joineR) data(heart.valve) heart.surv <- UniqueVariables(heart.valve,                                var.col = c("fuyrs", "status"),                               id.col = "num") heart.long <- heart.valve[, c("num", "time", "log.lvmi")] heart.cov <- UniqueVariables(heart.valve,                               c("age", "hs", "sex"),                               id.col = "num") heart.valve.jd <- jointdata(longitudinal = heart.long,                              baseline = heart.cov,                              survival = heart.surv,                              id.col = "num",                              time.col = "time") fit <- joint(data = heart.valve.jd,               long.formula = log.lvmi ~ 1 + time + hs,               surv.formula = Surv(fuyrs, status) ~ hs,               model = "intslope")  summary(fit) #>  #> Call: #> joint(data = heart.valve.jd, long.formula = log.lvmi ~ 1 + time +  #>     hs, surv.formula = Surv(fuyrs, status) ~ hs, model = "intslope") #>  #> Random effects joint model #>  Data: heart.valve.jd  #>  Log-likelihood: -423.7073  #>  #> Longitudinal sub-model fixed effects: log.lvmi ~ 1 + time + hs                               #> (Intercept)        4.992709176 #> time              -0.006977116 #> hsStentless valve  0.055921729 #>  #> Survival sub-model fixed effects: Surv(fuyrs, status) ~ hs                            #> hsStentless valve 0.8106134 #>  #> Latent association:                  #> gamma_0 0.8476275 #>  #> Variance components: #>         U_0         U_1    Residual  #> 0.113583056 0.001765284 0.037074269  #>  #> Convergence at iteration: 9  #>  #> Number of observations: 988  #> Number of groups: 256

title: joineR: Joint Modelling of Repeated Measurements and Time-to-Event Data — joineR-package
text: Analysis repeated measurements time--event data via random effects joint models. Fits joint models proposed Henderson colleagues doi:10.1093/biostatistics/1.4.465  (single event time) Williamson colleagues (2008) doi:10.1002/sim.3451  (competing risks events time) single continuous repeated measure. time--event data modelled using (cause-specific) Cox proportional hazards regression model time-varying covariates. longitudinal outcome modelled using linear mixed effects model. association captured latent Gaussian process. model estimated using Expectation Maximization algorithm. plotting functions variogram also included. project funded Medical Research Council (Grant numbers G0400615 MR/M013227/1).

text: joint function fits joint model survival   longitudinal data. formulation similar Wulfsohn Tsiatis   (1997). linear mixed effects model assumed longitudinal data,   namely $$Y_i = X_{i1}(t_i)^T\beta_1 + D_i(t_i)^T U_i + \epsilon_i,$$ \(U_i\) vector random effects, \((U_{0i}, \ldots   U_{qi})\) whose length depends model chosen, .e. \(q = 1\)   random intercept model. \(D_i\) random effects covariate matrix,   time-dependent random intercept model.   \(X_{i1}\) longitudinal design matrix unit \(\),   \(t_i\) vector measurement times subject \(\).   Measurement error represented \(\epsilon_i\). Cox proportional hazards model adopted survival data, namely $$\lambda(t) = \lambda_0(t) \exp\{{X_{i2}(t)^T \beta_2 +   D_i(t)(\gamma^T U_i)}\}.$$ parameter \(\gamma\) determines level association   two processes. intercept slope model separate association   $$D_i(t) (\gamma^T U_i) = \gamma_0 U_{0i} + \gamma_1 U_{1i} t,$$ whereas proportional association $$D_i(t)(\gamma^T U_i) = \gamma (U_{0i} + U_{1i} t).$$ \(X_{i2}\) vector survival covariates unit \(\).   baseline hazard function \(\lambda_0(t)\). function uses EM algorithm estimate parameters joint   model. Starting values provided calls standard R functions   lme coxph   longitudinal survival components, respectively.

code: ## Standard joint model  data(heart.valve) heart.surv <- UniqueVariables(heart.valve,                               var.col = c("fuyrs", "status"),                               id.col = "num") heart.long <- heart.valve[, c("num", "time", "log.lvmi")] heart.cov <- UniqueVariables(heart.valve,                              c("age", "hs", "sex"),                              id.col = "num") heart.valve.jd <- jointdata(longitudinal = heart.long,                             baseline = heart.cov,                             survival = heart.surv,                             id.col = "num",                             time.col = "time") fit <- joint(data = heart.valve.jd,              long.formula = log.lvmi ~ 1 + time + hs,              surv.formula = Surv(fuyrs, status) ~ hs,              model = "intslope")  ## Competing risks joint model (same data as Williamson et al. 2008)  if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ data(epileptic) epileptic$interaction <- with(epileptic, time * (treat == "LTG")) longitudinal <- epileptic[, c(1:3, 13)] survival <- UniqueVariables(epileptic, c(4, 6), "id") baseline <- UniqueVariables(epileptic, "treat", "id") data <- jointdata(longitudinal = longitudinal,                   survival = survival,                   baseline = baseline,                   id.col = "id",                   time.col = "time")  fit2 <- joint(data = data,               long.formula = dose ~ time + treat + interaction,               surv.formula = Surv(with.time, with.status2) ~ treat,               longsep = FALSE, survsep = FALSE,               gpt = 3) summary(fit2) } # }

title: Simulate data from a joint model — simjoint
text: function simjoint simulates data joint model,   similar performed Henderson et al. (2000). works first   simulating longitudinal data possible follow-times using random   draws multivariate Gaussian random effects residual error   terms. Data can simulated assuming either random-intercepts   (model = "int") longitudinal sub-models;   random-intercepts random-slopes (model = "intslope");   quadratic random effects structures (model = "quad"). failure   times simulated proportional hazards time--event models, using   following methodologies:  model = "int" baseline hazard function specified   exponential distribution $$\lambda_0(t) = \exp{\theta_0}.$$ Simulation conditional known time-independent effects,   methodology Bender et al. (2005) used simulate failure time. model = "intslope" baseline hazard function specified   Gompertz distribution $$\lambda_0(t) = \exp{\theta_0 + \theta_1 t}.$$ usual representation Gompertz distribution, \(\theta_1\)   shape parameter, scale parameter equivalent   \(\exp(\theta_0)\). Simulation conditional predictable   (linear) time-varying process, methodology Austin (2012) used   simulate failure time. model="quad" baseline hazard function specified per   model="intslope". integration technique used two   cases complex quadratic (higher order) models, therefore use   different approach. note hazard function can written $$\lim_{dt \rightarrow 0} \lambda(t) dt = \lim_{dt \rightarrow 0}   P[t \le T \le t + dt | T \ge t].$$ simulation routine parameter gridstep acts \(dt\)   choose nominally small value, multiplies hazard   scaled hazard equivalent probability event   interval \((t, t + dt)\), equivalently \((t, t +   \)gridstep\()\). vector possible times set   individual, ranging 0 trunctime increments \(dt\) (  gridstep). failure probability time compared   independent \(U(0, 1)\) draw, probability exceed   random draw survival time set trunctime, otherwise   generated time vector candidate times. minimum   candidate times (.e. deem event first happened)   taken survival time.

title: Summarise a random effects joint model fit — summary.joint
code: data(heart.valve) heart.surv <- UniqueVariables(heart.valve,                               var.col = c("fuyrs","status"),                               id.col = "num") heart.long <- heart.valve[, c("num", "time", "log.lvmi")] heart.cov <- UniqueVariables(heart.valve,                              c("age", "hs", "sex"),                              id.col = "num") heart.valve.jd <- jointdata(longitudinal = heart.long,                             baseline = heart.cov,                             survival = heart.surv,                             id.col = "num",                             time.col = "time") fit <- joint(data = heart.valve.jd,              long.formula = log.lvmi ~ 1 + time + hs,              surv.formula = Surv(fuyrs,status) ~ hs,              model = "intslope") summary(fit) #>  #> Call: #> joint(data = heart.valve.jd, long.formula = log.lvmi ~ 1 + time +  #>     hs, surv.formula = Surv(fuyrs, status) ~ hs, model = "intslope") #>  #> Random effects joint model #>  Data: heart.valve.jd  #>  Log-likelihood: -423.7073  #>  #> Longitudinal sub-model fixed effects: log.lvmi ~ 1 + time + hs                               #> (Intercept)        4.992709176 #> time              -0.006977116 #> hsStentless valve  0.055921729 #>  #> Survival sub-model fixed effects: Surv(fuyrs, status) ~ hs                            #> hsStentless valve 0.8106134 #>  #> Latent association:                  #> gamma_0 0.8476275 #>  #> Variance components: #>         U_0         U_1    Residual  #> 0.113583056 0.001765284 0.037074269  #>  #> Convergence at iteration: 9  #>  #> Number of observations: 988  #> Number of groups: 256

title: joineR 1.2.9
text: emUpdate() emUpdateCR() now issue warning() non-convergence instead silently calling print(), making possible callers catch non-convergence programmatically. survst() longer unconditionally forces first subject’s censoring indicator 1 passing data coxph(). proper stop() raised events observed. rs index vector now computed correctly via findInterval(), clamping subjects precede first event time risk set 1 rather producing NA. survst() survstCR() now correctly index loglik[2] (fitted model log-likelihood) rather storing full length-2 vector $log.like. Bootstrap confidence intervals jointSE() now use quantile() instead manual sorted-index lookup, fixing --one error small n.boot values.

title: joineR 1.2.9
text: joint() jointSE() arguments gpt, lgpt, max., tol now explicit default values function signatures, replacing previous missing() pattern. Defaults visible auto-complete ?joint. jointSE() output now includes p-value column containing two-sided Wald p-values fixed-effect association parameters. Variance component rows (U_*, Residual) return NA Wald tests appropriate positive-constrained parameters. Closes #58. jointSE() confidence intervals now computed values n.boot. Previously, runs fewer 100 bootstrap samples silently returned 0 CI bounds; now empirical percentile CIs always returned warning() issued n.boot < 100 alert user intervals may unreliable. tidy() glance() methods now available joint objects, providing tidy -style interface. tidy() returns  parameter estimates (one row per term) optionally accepts  result add standard errors, Wald statistics, p-values, confidence intervals. glance() returns single-row summary model-level statistics including log-likelihood, AIC, BIC, number observations, number subjects, convergence status, EM iteration count. Closes #59. simjoint() longer prints progress line via cat() internal simdat() function. now emits message() simjoint() level, can suppressed suppressMessages(). survival moved Depends Imports. Surv re-exported existing user code require changes. Test coverage improved 89% 93%. Code formatted air.

title: joineR 1.2.9
text: Added pkgdown site Bootstrap 5, full dark mode (light switch), structured reference index. pkgdown.yaml GitHub Actions workflow builds deploys site GitHub Pages push release. Added render-readme.yaml GitHub Actions workflow automatically re-renders README.md README.Rmd README.Rmd changed. Updated R-CMD-check.yaml test-coverage.yaml GitHub Actions workflows use actions/checkout@v6, codecov-action@v7, upload-artifact@v7. DESCRIPTION URL field now includes pkgdown site, CRAN page, GitHub repository. README badges updated: removed defunct AppVeyor Depsy badges; added pkgdown deployment badge lifecycle (stable) badge. Updated README new MRC logo fixed badges. Fixed deprecated documentation joineR-package.R. Added reverse dependency checks. Removed dead commented-code blocks survst.R prepSurvData.R. Removed -op fd <- fd / sd <- sd assignments emUpdate(). internal helper sortJointData() (previously sort.dat() defined inside joint() every call) now file-level internal function, avoiding spurious S3 method consistency note R CMD check. Fixed copy-paste error @param ylab documentation jointplot() (labelled “x-axis”). Renamed variable t obs_time jointplot() avoid shadowing base::t(). Fixed typo “Expectation Maximization” → “” DESCRIPTION. Updated test suite: removed deprecated context() calls replaced expect_is() expect_s3_class() throughout. Fixed typo “seperate” → “separate” one test name. Enabled testthat edition 3.
